articles.title: Gujarat’s Cherrapunji’ Runs Dry: Women Climb 45-Foot Wells for Water Amid Blistering Heat
articles.search_term: (heatwave OR "heat wave" OR "severe heatwave" OR "extreme heat" OR "heat spell" OR "scorching heat" OR "blistering heat" OR "sweltering heat" OR "hot winds" OR loo OR "hot loo winds") Gujarat
articles.full_text:
Amid the Gujarat government's claims of delivering tap water to every household, images emerged from the tribal and remote Kaprada taluka in Valsad district in Gujarat that cast serious doubts upon these assertions. Moti Palsan village, situated on the Gujarat-Maharashtra border and often dubbed Gujarat’s "Cherrapunji" due to its exceptionally high rainfall, is currently struggling for every single drop of water amidst the scorching summer heat.
In the Berasta Faliya locality of the village, conditions have deteriorated to such an extent that women are compelled to risk their lives by descending into a 45-foot-deep well. According to government records, there are eight wells in this area, which has a population of approximately 1,200; however, as the summer heat intensified, nearly all of them have run dry.
Women Rely on Iron Ladders, Ropes, Rods to Fetch Water
The quest for water has now evolved from a mere inconvenience into a daily peril. Women rely on iron ladders, ropes, and metal rods to descend into the wells. In some instances, they are forced to scoop up the meager amount of water accumulated within the crevices of the rocks at the bottom of the wells and haul it up in their vessels.
According to locals, several women have slipped and sustained injuries while doing this, yet the situation remains unchanged.
Long Queues, Just For One Or Two Vessels of Water
As soon as dawn breaks in the village, long queues begin to form near the wells. Women, often accompanied by their young children, wait for hours under the scorching sun just to collect the water that has accumulated drop by drop within the well.
A single family often has to wait for one to two hours to secure just one or two vessels of water. This dire situation arises every year at the onset of summer and persists until the arrival of the monsoon rains.
The most pressing questions are now being raised regarding the Rs 586 crore "Astol Group Water Supply Scheme," which was launched specifically to provide drinking water to the remote regions of Kaprada and Dharampur.
Under this scheme, household taps were installed and pipelines were laid across the village; however, villagers allege that, to this day, not a single drop of water has flowed from these taps. The situation is such that, in many places, pipelines and taps have already begun to rust and deteriorate even before water has reached them. Observe the irony: the very villages where flood-like conditions prevail during the monsoon season are the ones forced to descend into perilous wells, veritable "wells of death" in the summer, merely to secure a few drops of water.
